summary: Italian Renaissance polymath. Known for the Mona Lisa, The Last Supper, Vitruvian Man, Virgin of the Rocks, Lady with an Ermine, and The Battle of Anghiari. Used sfumato technique. Notebooks written in mirror script, including the Codex Leicester (owned by Bill Gates). Student of Verrocchio; teacher of Salai.

works.name: Mona Lisa
works.description: Portrait of a female member of the Giocondo family, using sfumato. At the Louvre. May depict a feminized self-portrait. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Portrait of a female member of the Giocondo family using sfumato technique; at the Louvre in its own room
examples: incorporated sfumato in his portrait of a female member of the Giocondo family
draws millions of visitors per year to its private room at the Louvre

works.name: Virgin of the Rocks
works.description: Two versions (Louvre and National Gallery London). Shows John the Baptist as a child kneeling beside a blue-cloaked Mary in a grotto. Uses sfumato. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Two versions (Louvre and London); shows John the Baptist as a child kneeling beside blue-cloaked Mary in a grotto
examples: The Louvre and London's National Gallery both house versions of a painting showing John the Baptist as a child kneeling beside a blue-cloaked Mary in a grotto

works.name: Vitruvian Man
works.description: Drawing of an idealized human figure inside a square and circle, inspired by Vitruvius's De architectura. Notes include 'six palms make one cubit.' Wikipedia

works.name: The Battle of Anghiari (lost)
works.description: Lost painting. Four soldiers fight for possession of a standard. May have been painted over by Vasari's Battle of Marciano (phrase 'Cerca Trova'). May have influenced Rubens's Hippopotamus Hunt.

clues.clue: Lost painting; four soldiers fight for a standard; may have been painted over by Vasari (phrase 'Cerca Trova'); Rubens emulated it
examples: Maurizio Seracini theorized that, based on the phrase 'Cerca Trova', Giorgio Vasari's fresco was painted on top of a work by this artist
Four soldiers fight for possession of a standard in a work by this artist

works.name: Lady with an Ermine (Cecilia Gallerani)
works.description: Portrait of Cecilia Gallerani holding an ermine, symbolizing her pregnancy.

works.name: General / Biographical
works.description: How Leonardo is identified and biographical clues.

clues.clue: Student of Verrocchio; modeled as the Archangel Raphael in Verrocchio's Tobias and the Angel
examples: modeled as the Archangel Raphael in the painting Tobias and the Angel by his mentor Verrocchio

clues.clue: Teacher of Salai; homoerotic drawing of Incarnate Angel modeled on Salai; John the Baptist modeled on Salai
examples: homoerotic drawing of an Incarnate Angel with an erect penis depicting the same student who modeled for his painting of John the Baptist

clues.clue: Ingres painted this man's deathbed being attended by King Francis I

comprehensive_summary:
Leonardo da Vinci (1452-1519) was an Italian Renaissance polymath, painter, and inventor. His Mona Lisa, a portrait of a member of the Giocondo family using his signature sfumato technique, is the most famous painting in the world and draws millions to its room at the Louvre. The Virgin of the Rocks exists in two versions (Louvre and National Gallery London), showing John the Baptist as a child kneeling beside a blue-cloaked Mary in a grotto.

The Vitruvian Man draws an idealized human figure inside a square and circle, inspired by Vitruvius's De architectura. The Battle of Anghiari is a lost painting of four soldiers fighting for a standard, possibly painted over by Vasari's Battle of Marciano (identified by the phrase 'Cerca Trova'). Lady with an Ermine depicts Cecilia Gallerani holding an ermine symbolizing her pregnancy. His portrait of Ginevra de' Benci is the only Leonardo publicly viewable in the Americas.

His notebooks, over 7,000 pages written in left-handed mirror script, include the Codex Leicester (owned by Bill Gates since 1994) and were inherited by Francesco Melzi. He was a student of Verrocchio and teacher of Salai. He was modeled as Plato in Raphael's School of Athens. Ingres depicted his death attended by King Francis I.
